questions.question: Explain the Two-Phase Commit protocol with its two phases.
keyPoints: Vote Phase: Coordinator sends VOTE-REQUEST, participants vote COMMIT or ABORT
Decision Phase: Coordinator makes global decision based on votes
If all vote COMMIT → GLOBAL-COMMIT sent to all
If any votes ABORT → GLOBAL-ABORT sent to all
Participants execute the decision and release locks

questions.question: What is the blocking problem in Two-Phase Commit? How does Three-Phase Commit address it?
keyPoints: Blocking problem: When coordinator fails after participants vote COMMIT, participants block waiting for decision
Participants cannot release locks or proceed
3PC solution: Adds PreCommit phase
If coordinator fails in PreCommit, participants can communicate
Participants know decision was COMMIT and proceed to DoCommit
Non-blocking protocol even during coordinator failures

questions.question: Explain the three phases of Three-Phase Commit protocol.
keyPoints: CanCommit: Coordinator asks participants if they can commit, participants reply YES/NO
PreCommit: If all YES, coordinator sends PRE-COMMIT, participants acknowledge
DoCommit: After receiving acknowledgments, coordinator sends DO-COMMIT, participants actually commit
If any NO in CanCommit, coordinator sends ABORT
If coordinator fails in any phase, participants can determine state and act

questions.question: Describe how timeout is handled in Two-Phase Commit protocol.
keyPoints: Coordinator timeout waiting for votes: Sends GLOBAL-ABORT to all
Participant timeout waiting for VOTE-REQUEST: Can abort locally
Participant timeout waiting for GLOBAL-DECISION: Blocks until decision arrives
If participant voted COMMIT and times out: Must wait (blocks)
Cannot unilaterally decide to commit or abort
Recovery from timeout requires coordinator recovery or manual intervention

questions.question: Explain the role of write-ahead logging in atomic commit protocols.
keyPoints: Write-ahead logging: Undo and Redo logs written before voting
Undo logs: Information to rollback changes if transaction aborts
Redo logs: Information to replay changes if transaction commits
Participants must write prepare log before voting COMMIT
Ensures durability and ability to recover after failures
If coordinator fails, participants use logs to determine appropriate action on recovery

questions.question: Compare Two-Phase Commit and Three-Phase Commit protocols in detail. Also explain why 3PC is preferred in certain scenarios.
keyPoints: 2PC has Vote and Decision phases; 3PC adds PreCommit phase
2PC blocks on coordinator failure; 3PC is non-blocking
2PC: 2 rounds of messaging; 3PC: 3 rounds of messaging
3PC has higher latency due to extra phase
2PC simpler; 3PC more complex but fault-tolerant
3PC preferred when: coordinator failure likely, high availability needed, long-running transactions
3PC not widely used in practice due to network partition issues
2PC widely implemented in databases due to simplicity

questions.question: Explain the complete operation of Two-Phase Commit when: (a) All participants vote COMMIT (b) One participant votes ABORT. Also discuss failure scenarios.
keyPoints: Case (a) All COMMIT: Vote request → all vote COMMIT → coordinator sends GLOBAL-COMMIT → all participants commit → release locks
Case (b) One ABORT: Vote request → any votes ABORT → coordinator sends GLOBAL-ABORT → all participants abort → undo changes
Failure: Coordinator fails after voting: Participants block waiting for decision
Failure: Participant fails before voting: Coordinator times out and aborts
Failure: Participant fails after voting commit: Upon recovery, checks log and commits
Network partition during decision: Some may commit, some block → inconsistent unless handled
Recovery: Use write-ahead logs to determine transaction state

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of atomic commit protocols. How do they ensure consistency in distributed databases?
keyPoints: Advantages: Guarantees all-or-nothing atomicity, maintains data consistency across nodes, provides isolation through locking
Disadvantages: 2PC blocks on coordinator failure, latency due to multiple rounds, all participants must be available
3PC advantages: Non-blocking, better failure handling; Disadvantages: More messages, complex, network partition issues
Consistency: Ensures either all nodes apply changes or none do
No partial commits or partial aborts possible
Sequential consistency maintained across distributed database
Distributed snapshot isolation possible with these protocols
Used in distributed databases, distributed transactions, microservices
